WCAG in a nutshell

Let's dive into the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines

Anna Kocaj

2/17/2026 · 2 min read

Why should I care?

You could say, you do not target your products at people with disabilities. But studies shows that 1 in 6 of us expirience significant disability [1]. Even those of us with perfect health might find some online information hard to access. So, why not make our lives easier?

Web Content Accessibility Guidelines

The WWW stardards

Did you hear about W3C? It is a non-profit organization that develops internet standards like HTML or CCS. Their mission is “Making the web work — for everyone.” [2] To provide developers and designers with a guide to create better websites, they came up with first WCAG in 1999!

WCAG versions

WCAG 2.0 was a major update in 2008 that introduced the 4 main principles as we know it now. Later in 2018 it was expanded to cover mobile device needs in version 2.1. The lastest update, WCAG 2.2 in 2023, is a base to many directives all around the world. At the moment W3C is working on WCAG 3.0 that is promised to be an easier to understand and more flexible guide.

POUR principles

WCAG is based on 4 words:

  • Perceivable
  • Operable
  • Understandable
  • Robust

Each word represents a different principle. All of them feature 87 success criteria with 3 levels: A, AA and AAA.

Where to find the latest WCAG?

It is best to use the official W3C website. What is nice to know, WCAG is translated to many languages.

WCAG equivalents/interpretations across the world

Getting familiar with other guides than WCAG is a good way of undertanding the problems better:

  • French RGAA (Référentiel général d’amélioration de l’accessibilité)
  • German BITV (Barrierefreie-Informationstechnik-Verordnung)
  • American Section 508

As a designer I feel responsible for making web more accessible. Aside from the legal point of view, I’ve seen many people struggle interacting with content online. Making their life a bit easier feels like a good purpose for my career.

In the next posts I’m going to share my tips for following WCAG rules, as it is my current learning focus.

References

  1. https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/disability-and-health
  2. https://www.w3.org/mission/
